title: Dispersion of multiple charged species in an axially symmetric slowly varying channel
authors: Thakurdas Mahata, Anirban Chatterjee, Ameeya Kumar Nayak
abstract: The transport and dispersion of multiple species of charged ions are central to many biological and physical processes, including electrokinetic ion separation. However, most theoretical studies of dispersion in channels have focused on neutral solutes, leaving the transport of multiple charged species comparatively unexplored. Differences in ionic diffusivities in a multispecies electrolyte solution generate an self-induced electric fields that drive electromigration. To capture these effects at the macroscopic scale, we combine the lubrication approximation with homogenization theory, under electroneutrality and zero-current constraints, to derive an effective transport equation governing the cross-sectionally averaged concentrations. We apply our model framework to a range of channel geometries and compute the resulting effective dispersion coefficients. Finally, we investigate how channel geometry can be tuned to enhance ionic separation. We observe a geometry-induced electro-diffusive coupling that inhibits solute dispersion in certain channels, leading to a non-monotonic Number of Theoretical Plates (NTP) and making such channels ideal for separation processes.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to describe how the shape of a microchannel can be used to control the dispersion and separation of different ions. By leveraging geometry-induced electric fields, the authors show that certain channel configurations can significantly enhance the efficiency of ionic separation processes.
main_problem: Investigating the dispersion and separation efficiency of multiple charged ionic species in axially symmetric, slowly varying microchannels, specifically looking at how channel geometry influences electro-diffusive coupling.
main_result: The study discovers a geometry-induced electro-diffusive coupling that can inhibit solute dispersion, leading to non-monotonic behavior in separation efficiency (NTP) and demonstrating that channel geometry can be tuned to enhance ionic separation.
method: The authors use lubrication approximation, multiple-scale perturbation theory, and homogenization theory to derive a reduced-order macrotransport equation, which is then validated via numerical simulations using the Method of Lines.
model_or_system: An electrolyte solution containing N different ionic species flowing through an axisymmetric channel with a slowly varying radius, governed by the Nernst-Planck and Navier-Stokes equations under electroneutrality and zero-current constraints.
key_observables: Effective dispersion coefficients (k_eff), Number of Theoretical Plates (NTP), and transverse-averaged concentration profiles.
important_parameters: Peclet number (Pe), wall slope/geometry (beta), ionic valency (z_i), ionic diffusivity (k_i), and the arc-to-chord ratio (Lambda).
main_assumptions: The channel is axially symmetric and slowly varying; the system is in the low-Reynolds-number regime; electroneutrality and zero net current are enforced; and the model is valid at the long-time diffusion scale.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of ion dispersion; Figures 2-5 illustrate concentration profiles and effective diffusivity evolution for sinusoidal, triangular, and converging/diverging geometries; Figures 7-9 show the dependence of separation efficiency (N) and cumulative electric effects on geometric parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multi-species dispersion, presents a mathematical framework using perturbation and homogenization methods, applies the model to various channel geometries (sinusoidal, triangular, converging, diverging), and concludes with an analysis of separation efficiency optimization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic diagram of dispersion of multiple charged species under an induced electric ﬁeld, driven by a Poiseuille ﬂow.
Figure 2: A comparison between the numerical results obtained in the present work (solid lines) and the results published by [23] (dashed lines), shown for time t=2.

Figure 3: Schematic representation of the sinusoidal channel geometry with diﬀerent periodic lengths, l (a) l = 8π (two period), (b) l = 4π (four period), and (c) l = 2π (eight period), with the inlet height a0 and amplitude δ = 0.5. (d-e) Represents the transverse-averaged concentration ﬁelds along the streamwise direction at time t = 8 for Pe = 2 and Q = 1 corresponding to diﬀerent periodic lengths. (g-i) Represents temporal evaluation of eﬀective tne diﬀusivities keff,i, i = 1, 2, 3 corresponding to the ﬁrst, second, and third ionic species, respectively, in diﬀerent periodic lengths.
